title: Why Gradients Vanish in Recurrence
concept: If the recurrent weights are slightly less than 1, multiplying them repeatedly across many steps causes the gradient signal to shrink exponentially to zero.

exercises.prompt: What type of data are Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) mainly designed to process?

options.text: Static database tables with no time order

exercises.explain: Recurrent Neural Networks process sequential data like text or time-series by passing information from one step to the next.

cards.title: Forgotten Beginnings
cards.body: Have you ever read a long paragraph and completely forgotten how it started? Recurrent Neural Networks face the exact same problem! When sentences get long, their memory of the early words fades away. Let's find out why.

cards.title: Vanishing Gradients
cards.body: Vanishing gradients happen in Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) because we multiply the same weight repeatedly across many time steps. If that recurrent weight is slightly less than 1, the gradient (the signal used to learn) shrinks exponentially until it disappears.

cards.title: The Whisper Chain
cards.body: Imagine whispering a secret down a line of 100 people. If each person passes on only 90% of the volume they heard, by the time the message reaches the end, it will dwindle to a silent breath. In recurrent networks, the 'secret' is the gradient, and the whispering line is the sequence of steps.

cards.title: A Long Sentence
cards.body: Suppose you feed a 50-word sentence into an RNN. To calculate how the first word affects the final output, the network multiplies the connection weight 50 times (e.g., 0.9 to the power of 50). This results in a number so tiny (0.005) that the network completely forgets the beginning of the sentence!

cards.title: It's Not a Hardware Issue
cards.body: You might think that using computers with higher precision will solve this. But no matter how precise your computer is, multiplying a fraction by itself dozens of times will mathematically shrink it to near-zero. We need structural changes, not just better hardware, to fix it.

cards.title: Gradient Flow Comparison
cards.body: Here is how weight values change the gradient signal as it travels backward through time.

right.text: The signal remains stable and flows all the way back.

left.text: The signal shrinks exponentially and vanishes quickly.

exercises.prompt: Which recurrent weight value is most likely to cause vanishing gradients over many steps?
exercises.explain: A weight less than 1.0 (like 0.9) gets smaller and smaller the more times it is multiplied by itself, leading to vanishing gradients.

exercises.prompt: Repeatedly multiplying a number that is less than 1 causes it to shrink toward zero.
exercises.explain: True! Multiplying a number less than 1 over and over makes it shrink exponentially, which is the mathematical root of the vanishing gradient problem.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe why recurrent networks lose early information.
exercises.sentence: Gradients vanish because the recurrent weights are repeatedly [blank] across sequence steps.
exercises.explain: Because RNNs reuse the same weight at each step, gradients get multiplied repeatedly, causing them to shrink rapidly if the weight is less than 1.

exercises.prompt: Which AI assistant makes a false claim about recurrent networks?

options.text: AI Model A: The gradient shrinks to zero because multiplying small weights repeatedly reduces the signal.
AI Model B: The gradient will explode to infinity because recurrent weights under 1.0 amplify the signals over time.
AI Model C: The gradient disappears because the early steps get diluted by the repetitive multiplications.

exercises.explain: The AI response is incorrect because multiplying numbers less than 1 repeatedly causes them to shrink (vanish), not grow (explode).

exercises.prompt: If a recurrent neural network processes a sequence of 100 words, what happens to the gradients at the very beginning of the sentence if the recurrent weight is 0.9?

options.text: They stay exactly the same size
They fluctuate up and down randomly

exercises.explain: Multiplying any number less than 1 (like 0.9) over and over again causes it to shrink exponentially close to zero, making it impossible to update the earliest steps in a long sequence.
